title: String Functions 🛠️
description: Copy, compare, and measure string data.

slides.title: Using the string.h Library 📚
slides.content:
Because strings are actually arrays, you CANNOT do this:
`string1 = string2;` (You can't copy an entire array like that in C!)

Instead, you have to include the `<string.h>` library at the top of your program. It provides functions to manipulate text safely.

slides.title: 1. Length: strlen()
slides.content: `strlen()` counts the number of characters in a string (ignoring the invisible null terminator).

slides.title: 2. Copy: strcpy()
slides.content: If you want to copy the contents of one string into another, use `strcpy(destination, source)`. It copies character by character.

quizData.question: Why didn't we just write `destination = source;`?

options.text: Because they are actually memory arrays, and C doesn't allow direct array assignment.
Because `=` is only used for integers.
It would actually delete the source.

quizData.explanation: Arrays in C cannot be copied with `=`. You have to loop through and copy each slot individually (which is what `strcpy` does for you under the hood).

slides.title: 3. Compare: strcmp() ⚖️
slides.content:
You also CANNOT use `==` to see if two strings are the same word.

Use `strcmp(str1, str2)`. If it evaluates to exactly `0`, the two strings are identical.

quizData.question: If you use `if(strcmp(password, "12345") == 0)`, what does a result of 0 mean?

options.text: It means the password is wrong (False).
It means the passwords differ by 0 characters—they are an exact match!
It means the password is too short.

quizData.explanation: `strcmp` returns the alphabetical difference between the words. If there is 0 difference, they match perfectly.
